\begin{document}

\begin{center}
    {\LARGE \bfseries Systems of Linear Differential Equations}\\[6pt]
    {\large Prepared by Staples Education}\\[4pt]
    {\itshape Unit 15: Decoupling, the Matrix Exponential, and Fundamental Matrices}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4em}

\tableofcontents
\vspace{0.4em}

\begin{topicsbox}[Unit 15 at a Glance: Solving Coupled Linear Systems]
A coupled system $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ looks tangled only because the variables are written in the wrong basis. The eigenvectors of $A$ are the right basis: in those coordinates the matrix becomes diagonal, $A=PDP^{-1}$, and the substitution $\mathbf{y}=P^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ \emph{decouples} the system into independent scalar equations $\mathbf{y}'=D\mathbf{y}$. Packaging all of the modes into one operator gives the matrix exponential $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$, the exact matrix-valued analogue of the scalar $e^{at}$, whose columns are collected by the fundamental matrix.

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.4em, itemsep=3pt]
    \item \textbf{Higher order to first order} --- any $n$th-order linear ODE becomes a first-order system $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ through the state vector of derivatives.
    \item \textbf{Decoupling by diagonalization} --- with distinct eigenvalues, $A=PDP^{-1}$ and $\mathbf{y}=P^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ split the system into independent modes $y_i'=\lambda_i y_i$.
    \item \textbf{The matrix exponential} --- $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$ solves $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x},\ \mathbf{x}(0)=\mathbf{x}_0$ as $\mathbf{x}(t)=e^{At}\mathbf{x}_0$, generalizing scalar $e^{at}$.
    \item \textbf{Fundamental matrices} --- $\Psi(t)=[\mathbf{x}_1\mid\mathbf{x}_2\mid\cdots]$ collects independent solutions; $\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}=e^{At}$.
    \item \textbf{Complex eigenvalues} --- $\lambda=\alpha\pm i\beta$ produce spiraling real modes $e^{\alpha t}(\cos\beta t,\ \sin\beta t)$ through Euler's formula.
\end{itemize}
\end{topicsbox}

\vspace{0.6em}

\setcounter{section}{15}

% ---- Module 15.1 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Converting Higher-Order Equations to First-Order Systems}
\emph{Recasting an $n$th-order linear ODE as a first-order system; the state vector of successive derivatives; the companion matrix; the unified matrix form $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$.}

\begin{formulabox}[The State-Vector Substitution]
\[ x_1=y,\ x_2=y',\ \ldots,\ x_n=y^{(n-1)} \;\Rightarrow\; x_1'=x_2,\ x_2'=x_3,\ \ldots,\ x_{n-1}'=x_n. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Companion Form of $y''+py'+qy=0$]
\[ \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix}' = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -q & -p \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad x_1=y,\ x_2=y'. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[The Unified Matrix Form]
\[ \mathbf{x}' = A\mathbf{x}, \qquad \mathbf{x}=\big(x_1,\,x_2,\,\ldots,\,x_n\big)^{\!\top}, \qquad A \text{ constant } n\times n. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Convert an $n$th-Order ODE to a System]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Name the state variables $x_1=y,\ x_2=y',\ \ldots,\ x_n=y^{(n-1)}$.
    \item Differentiate each: the first $n-1$ rows are simply $x_i'=x_{i+1}$.
    \item Solve the original ODE for the top derivative $y^{(n)}$ and write the last row $x_n'$ in terms of the $x_i$.
    \item Collect the coefficients into the companion matrix $A$ and read off $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- A Second-Order ODE Becomes a System]
Convert $y'' + 5y' + 6y = 0$ into a first-order system $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\text{Let } x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = y'. \text{ Then } x_1' = y' = x_2.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{x_2' = y'' = -5y' - 6y = -6x_1 - 5x_2.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix}' = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\text{Check: } \det(A-\lambda I) = \lambda^2 + 5\lambda + 6 = (\lambda+2)(\lambda+3) \text{ matches the ODE's characteristic polynomial.}} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
Every higher-order linear ODE is secretly a first-order \textcolor{accentorange}{system}. The state vector stacks $y$ together with its derivatives, and the companion matrix's characteristic polynomial is \textcolor{accentorange}{exactly the characteristic equation of the original ODE} --- so the eigenvalue machinery and the scalar method agree.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
When you solve for the top derivative, the coefficients enter the last row of $A$ with \textcolor{warnred}{flipped signs}: $y''=-6y-5y'$ gives the row $(-6,\,-5)$, not $(6,\,5)$. Forgetting the sign flip silently changes every eigenvalue.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 15.2 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{The Eigenvalue Method and Decoupling}
\emph{The trial solution $\mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$; the modal matrix $P$ of eigenvectors and the diagonal eigenvalue matrix $D$; the diagonalization $A=PDP^{-1}$; the modal substitution $\mathbf{y}=P^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ that turns $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ into the decoupled $\mathbf{y}'=D\mathbf{y}$.}

\begin{formulabox}[Eigen-Ansatz and the General Solution]
\[ \mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}\ \Rightarrow\ A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}; \qquad \mathbf{x}(t)=c_1 e^{\lambda_1 t}\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}\mathbf{v}_2. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Modal Matrix and Diagonalization]
\[ P = \big[\,\mathbf{v}_1 \mid \mathbf{v}_2\,\big], \quad D = \begin{pmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 \end{pmatrix}: \qquad A = PDP^{-1}, \qquad D = P^{-1}AP. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[The Decoupling Substitution]
\[ \mathbf{y}=P^{-1}\mathbf{x} \ \Rightarrow\ \mathbf{y}' = P^{-1}\mathbf{x}' = P^{-1}A\mathbf{x} = P^{-1}AP\,\mathbf{y} = D\mathbf{y}: \quad y_i' = \lambda_i y_i. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Decouple $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ into $\mathbf{y}'=D\mathbf{y}$]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Solve $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$ for the eigenvalues $\lambda_1,\lambda_2$ and find an eigenvector $\mathbf{v}_i$ for each.
    \item Build the modal matrix $P=[\mathbf{v}_1\mid\mathbf{v}_2]$ from the eigenvectors as columns and the diagonal $D=\operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)$.
    \item Compute $P^{-1}$ and confirm the diagonalization $P^{-1}AP=D$ (this is the algebraic heart of the decoupling).
    \item Substitute $\mathbf{y}=P^{-1}\mathbf{x}$: the system collapses to the independent modes $y_i'=\lambda_i y_i$, solve each as $y_i=c_i e^{\lambda_i t}$, then return via $\mathbf{x}=P\mathbf{y}$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- Decoupling a System with Distinct Real Eigenvalues]
Decouple $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ for $A=\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute the eigenvalues, the modal matrix $P$, its inverse $P^{-1}$, and verify $P^{-1}AP=D$ in full --- no skipped steps.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Eigenvalues: } \det(A-\lambda I)=\det\begin{pmatrix} 1-\lambda & 2 \\ 3 & 2-\lambda \end{pmatrix} = (1-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - (2)(3).} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{(1-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - 6 = \lambda^2 - 3\lambda + 2 - 6 = \lambda^2 - 3\lambda - 4 = (\lambda-4)(\lambda+1).} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda^2 - 3\lambda - 4 = 0 \ \Rightarrow\ \lambda_1 = 4, \quad \lambda_2 = -1.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Eigenvector } \lambda_1=4:\ (A-4I)\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} -3 & 2 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} \ \Rightarrow\ -3v_1 + 2v_2 = 0 \ \Rightarrow\ \mathbf{v}_1 = (2,\,3).} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Eigenvector } \lambda_2=-1:\ (A+I)\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 3 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} \ \Rightarrow\ v_1 + v_2 = 0 \ \Rightarrow\ \mathbf{v}_2 = (1,\,-1).} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Modal matrix: } P = \big[\,\mathbf{v}_1 \mid \mathbf{v}_2\,\big] = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad D = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Inverse: } \det P = (2)(-1) - (1)(3) = -5, \qquad P^{-1} = \frac{1}{-5}\begin{pmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ -3 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Verify } P^{-1}P=I:\ \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 \end{pmatrix} = I.\ \checkmark} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{First product } AP = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 8 & -1 \\ 12 & 1 \end{pmatrix}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Then } P^{-1}(AP) = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 8 & -1 \\ 12 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 20 & 0 \\ 0 & -5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = D.\ \checkmark} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Decoupled system: } \mathbf{y}'=D\mathbf{y} \ \Rightarrow\ y_1'=4y_1,\ \ y_2'=-y_2 \ \Rightarrow\ y_1=c_1 e^{4t},\ y_2=c_2 e^{-t}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Back-substitute } \mathbf{x}=P\mathbf{y}:\ \mathbf{x}(t) = c_1 e^{4t}\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} + c_2 e^{-t}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}.} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
Diagonalization \emph{is} decoupling. Writing $A=PDP^{-1}$ and changing to modal coordinates $\mathbf{y}=P^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ replaces the tangled $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ with $n$ \textcolor{accentorange}{independent scalar equations} $y_i'=\lambda_i y_i$. Each mode evolves as $e^{\lambda_i t}$ on its own eigenvector, and $\mathbf{x}=P\mathbf{y}$ reassembles them.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
The eigenvectors are the \textcolor{warnred}{columns} of $P$, in the same order as the eigenvalues down the diagonal of $D$. Swapping a column without swapping the matching diagonal entry breaks $P^{-1}AP=D$. Diagonalization also \textcolor{warnred}{requires a full set of independent eigenvectors} --- a repeated eigenvalue short on eigenvectors is defective and needs the generalized-eigenvector route instead.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 15.3 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{The Matrix Exponential}
\emph{The power-series definition of $e^{At}$; its closed form $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$ via diagonalization; $e^{Dt}=\operatorname{diag}(e^{\lambda_i t})$; why $e^{At}$ is the natural matrix generalization of the scalar $e^{at}$; the initial-value solution $\mathbf{x}(t)=e^{At}\mathbf{x}_0$.}

\begin{formulabox}[Definition by the Exponential Series]
\[ e^{At} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(At)^k}{k!} = I + At + \frac{(At)^2}{2!} + \frac{(At)^3}{3!} + \cdots, \qquad \frac{d}{dt}e^{At} = A\,e^{At}. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Closed Form via Diagonalization]
\[ A = PDP^{-1} \ \Rightarrow\ e^{At} = P\,e^{Dt}\,P^{-1}, \qquad e^{Dt} = \begin{pmatrix} e^{\lambda_1 t} & 0 \\ 0 & e^{\lambda_2 t} \end{pmatrix}. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Generalization of the Scalar $e^{at}$]
\[ \text{scalar: } x'=ax \Rightarrow x=e^{at}x_0; \qquad \text{matrix: } \mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x} \Rightarrow \mathbf{x}(t)=e^{At}\mathbf{x}_0, \quad e^{A\cdot 0}=I. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Compute $e^{At}$ by Diagonalization]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Diagonalize $A=PDP^{-1}$ using the eigenvectors (columns of $P$) and eigenvalues (diagonal of $D$).
    \item Exponentiate the diagonal entrywise: $e^{Dt}=\operatorname{diag}(e^{\lambda_1 t},\,e^{\lambda_2 t})$, since the series of a diagonal matrix acts independently on each entry.
    \item Reassemble $e^{At}=P\,e^{Dt}\,P^{-1}$ by multiplying the three matrices left to right.
    \item Sanity check $e^{A\cdot 0}=I$, then read off the IVP solution $\mathbf{x}(t)=e^{At}\mathbf{x}_0$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- The Matrix Exponential of the Same $A$]
Using the diagonalization $A=\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}=PDP^{-1}$ from 15.2 (with $P=\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$, $P^{-1}=\tfrac15\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$, $D=\operatorname{diag}(4,-1)$), build $e^{At}$ explicitly.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Exponentiate the diagonal: } e^{Dt} = \begin{pmatrix} e^{4t} & 0 \\ 0 & e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{First product } P\,e^{Dt} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} e^{4t} & 0 \\ 0 & e^{-t} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t} & e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t} & -e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Then } e^{At} = \big(P\,e^{Dt}\big)P^{-1} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t} & e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t} & -e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{e^{At} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t} + 3e^{-t} & 2e^{4t} - 2e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t} - 3e^{-t} & 3e^{4t} + 2e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Check } t=0:\ e^{A\cdot 0} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 2+3 & 2-2 \\ 3-3 & 3+2 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 \end{pmatrix} = I.\ \checkmark} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{IVP solution: } \mathbf{x}(t) = e^{At}\mathbf{x}_0 \text{ delivers the trajectory through any } \mathbf{x}_0 \text{ in one stroke.}} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
The matrix exponential is the \textcolor{accentorange}{single operator that advances the whole system in time}. Because $A$ and $P$ share eigenvectors, $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$ simply exponentiates each eigenvalue and reassembles --- the exact matrix analogue of $e^{at}$, satisfying $\frac{d}{dt}e^{At}=Ae^{At}$ and $e^{A\cdot 0}=I$, so $\mathbf{x}(t)=e^{At}\mathbf{x}_0$ solves the IVP outright.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
$e^{At}$ is \textcolor{warnred}{not} the entrywise exponential of $At$ --- you must use the series or the diagonalization. And $e^{At}e^{Bt}=e^{(A+B)t}$ holds \textcolor{warnred}{only when $A$ and $B$ commute} ($AB=BA$); for non-commuting matrices that shortcut fails.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 15.4 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Fundamental Matrices and the General Solution}
\emph{The fundamental matrix $\Psi(t)$ whose columns are independent solutions; the Wronskian as its determinant; the compact general solution $\mathbf{x}=\Psi(t)\mathbf{c}$; the link $e^{At}=\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}$ that normalizes any fundamental matrix to the exponential.}

\begin{formulabox}[Fundamental Matrix and General Solution]
\[ \Psi(t) = \big[\,\mathbf{x}_1(t) \mid \mathbf{x}_2(t)\,\big], \qquad \Psi'(t) = A\,\Psi(t), \qquad \mathbf{x}(t) = \Psi(t)\,\mathbf{c}. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Independence via the Wronskian]
\[ W(t) = \det\Psi(t) \neq 0 \ \text{ for some } t \ \Rightarrow\ \text{the columns are linearly independent solutions.} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Normalization to the Matrix Exponential]
\[ e^{At} = \Psi(t)\,\Psi(0)^{-1}, \qquad e^{At}\big|_{t=0} = \Psi(0)\Psi(0)^{-1} = I. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Build and Use a Fundamental Matrix]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Find a full set of independent solutions $\mathbf{x}_i(t)=e^{\lambda_i t}\mathbf{v}_i$ from the eigenpairs.
    \item Place them as the columns of $\Psi(t)=[\mathbf{x}_1\mid\mathbf{x}_2\mid\cdots]$ and confirm $\det\Psi\neq 0$.
    \item Write the general solution compactly as $\mathbf{x}(t)=\Psi(t)\mathbf{c}$, fixing $\mathbf{c}$ from $\mathbf{x}(0)=\Psi(0)\mathbf{c}$.
    \item If the transition operator is wanted, normalize: $e^{At}=\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- Fundamental Matrix Recovers $e^{At}$]
For $A=\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ with eigensolutions $e^{4t}(2,3)$ and $e^{-t}(1,-1)$, build $\Psi(t)$ and confirm $\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}=e^{At}$.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\Psi(t) = \begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t} & e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t} & -e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}, \qquad W(t)=\det\Psi = -2e^{3t} - 3e^{3t} = -5e^{3t} \neq 0.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\Psi(0) = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = P, \qquad \Psi(0)^{-1} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix} = P^{-1}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t} & e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t} & -e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}\cdot\frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t}+3e^{-t} & 2e^{4t}-2e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t}-3e^{-t} & 3e^{4t}+2e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\text{This is exactly the } e^{At} \text{ of 15.3: a fundamental matrix normalized at } t=0 \text{ is the matrix exponential.}\ \checkmark} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
The fundamental matrix is the \textcolor{accentorange}{bookkeeping device for an entire solution space}: its columns are independent solutions, $\det\Psi\neq 0$ guarantees they span, and $\mathbf{x}=\Psi\mathbf{c}$ is every solution at once. Normalizing it at $t=0$ recovers the matrix exponential, $e^{At}=\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}$ --- two languages for the same flow.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
Any $\Psi$ with independent columns is \emph{a} fundamental matrix, but it equals $e^{At}$ \textcolor{warnred}{only when $\Psi(0)=I$}. If $\Psi(0)\neq I$ you must multiply by $\Psi(0)^{-1}$ on the right --- not the left. Order matters: $\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}$, never $\Psi(0)^{-1}\Psi(t)$.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 15.5 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Complex Eigenvalues and Oscillatory Modes}
\emph{Complex conjugate eigenvalues $\lambda=\alpha\pm i\beta$; extracting two real solutions from one complex eigensolution via Euler's formula; the real general solution as decaying or growing oscillations $e^{\alpha t}(\cos\beta t,\ \sin\beta t)$.}

\begin{formulabox}[Complex Eigenpair and Euler Expansion]
\[ \lambda = \alpha \pm i\beta: \qquad e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v} = e^{\alpha t}\big(\cos\beta t + i\sin\beta t\big)\mathbf{v}. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Two Real Solutions from One Complex Mode]
\[ \mathbf{x}_1 = \operatorname{Re}\!\big(e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}\big), \qquad \mathbf{x}_2 = \operatorname{Im}\!\big(e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}\big); \qquad \mathbf{x} = c_1\mathbf{x}_1 + c_2\mathbf{x}_2. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Geometry from the Sign of $\alpha$]
\[ \alpha<0:\ \text{inward spiral}, \quad \alpha=0:\ \text{closed orbits (center)}, \quad \alpha>0:\ \text{outward spiral}. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Solve a System with Complex Eigenvalues]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Find the complex eigenvalue $\lambda=\alpha+i\beta$ and a complex eigenvector $\mathbf{v}$ (use only one of the conjugate pair).
    \item Form the complex solution $e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$ and expand with $e^{i\beta t}=\cos\beta t + i\sin\beta t$.
    \item Split into real and imaginary parts $\mathbf{x}_1=\operatorname{Re}$, $\mathbf{x}_2=\operatorname{Im}$ --- two independent real solutions.
    \item Write the real general solution $\mathbf{x}=c_1\mathbf{x}_1 + c_2\mathbf{x}_2$ and read the spiral direction from $\operatorname{sign}(\alpha)$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- A Spiraling System]
Solve $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ for $A=\begin{pmatrix} 2 & -3 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\det(A-\lambda I) = (2-\lambda)^2 + 9 = \lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 13 = 0 \ \Rightarrow\ \lambda = 2 \pm 3i \quad (\alpha=2,\ \beta=3).} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda = 2+3i:\ (A-\lambda I)\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} -3i & -3 \\ 3 & -3i \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} \ \Rightarrow\ \mathbf{v} = (1,\,-i).} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v} = e^{2t}\big(\cos 3t + i\sin 3t\big)\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -i \end{pmatrix} = e^{2t}\begin{pmatrix} \cos 3t + i\sin 3t \\ \sin 3t - i\cos 3t \end{pmatrix}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\mathbf{x}_1 = \operatorname{Re} = e^{2t}\begin{pmatrix} \cos 3t \\ \sin 3t \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{x}_2 = \operatorname{Im} = e^{2t}\begin{pmatrix} \sin 3t \\ -\cos 3t \end{pmatrix}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\mathbf{x}(t) = e^{2t}\left[\, c_1\begin{pmatrix} \cos 3t \\ \sin 3t \end{pmatrix} + c_2\begin{pmatrix} \sin 3t \\ -\cos 3t \end{pmatrix} \right]: \quad \alpha=2>0 \Rightarrow \text{outward spiral.}} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
A complex eigenpair encodes \textcolor{accentorange}{rotation plus scaling}. Euler's formula turns the single complex mode $e^{(\alpha+i\beta)t}\mathbf{v}$ into two real solutions whose $\cos\beta t$ and $\sin\beta t$ supply the oscillation and whose $e^{\alpha t}$ envelope sets the spiral: inward for $\alpha<0$, a closed center for $\alpha=0$, outward for $\alpha>0$.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
Use \textcolor{warnred}{only one} eigenvalue of the conjugate pair --- taking both double-counts the solution space. And the real and imaginary parts are \emph{already} the two independent real solutions; do not also tack on the conjugate $\overline{\lambda}$ mode.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

\clearpage
\phantomsection
\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Practice Problems}
\section*{Practice Problems}

Work the following ten problems in order. They climb from converting a higher-order ODE into a first-order system, through the eigenvalue method and the full decoupling $A=PDP^{-1}$ with $P$, $P^{-1}$, and $D$ computed explicitly, into the matrix exponential $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$, the fundamental matrix and its link $e^{At}=\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}$, and a complex-eigenvalue capstone that produces a spiraling real solution. Complete solutions for all problems follow.

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=2em]
    \item \textbf{(To a system)} Convert $y'' + 5y' + 6y = 0$ into a first-order system $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$, and state the companion matrix $A$.

    \item \textbf{(Eigenvalues)} For $A=\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, compute $\det(A-\lambda I)$ and find both eigenvalues.

    \item \textbf{(Eigenvectors and $P$)} For that same $A$, find an eigenvector for each eigenvalue and assemble the modal matrix $P=[\mathbf{v}_1\mid\mathbf{v}_2]$.

    \item \textbf{(Inverse)} Compute $P^{-1}$ for $P=\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$, showing $\det P$ explicitly.

    \item \textbf{(Verify decoupling)} Confirm $P^{-1}AP=D$ for the $A$, $P$, $P^{-1}$ above, and state the decoupled system $\mathbf{y}'=D\mathbf{y}$.

    \item \textbf{(General solution)} Write the general solution $\mathbf{x}(t)$ of $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ from the eigenpairs $\lambda_1=4,\ \mathbf{v}_1=(2,3)$ and $\lambda_2=-1,\ \mathbf{v}_2=(1,-1)$.

    \item \textbf{(Matrix exponential)} Using $A=PDP^{-1}$ with $D=\operatorname{diag}(4,-1)$, build $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$ in closed form.

    \item \textbf{(Fundamental matrix)} Write a fundamental matrix $\Psi(t)$ for this system and compute its Wronskian $\det\Psi(t)$.

    \item \textbf{(Normalization)} Explain why $e^{At}=\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}$, and identify $\Psi(0)$ for the $\Psi$ above.

    \item \textbf{(Complex capstone)} Solve $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ for $A=\begin{pmatrix} 2 & -3 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, giving the real general solution and the spiral direction.

\end{enumerate}

\clearpage
\phantomsection
\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Complete Solutions}
\section*{Complete Solutions}

\noindent\textbf{Solution 1.}\quad Let $x_1=y$, $x_2=y'$, so $x_1'=x_2$ and $x_2'=y''=-6y-5y'=-6x_1-5x_2$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix}' = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{pmatrix}.} \]
The coefficients enter the bottom row with flipped signs.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 2.}\quad Expand the determinant:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\det(A-\lambda I) = (1-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - (2)(3) = \lambda^2 - 3\lambda - 4 = (\lambda-4)(\lambda+1).} \]
So $\lambda_1=4$ and $\lambda_2=-1$.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 3.}\quad Solve $(A-\lambda I)\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$ for each eigenvalue:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda=4:\ \begin{pmatrix} -3 & 2 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0} \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_1=(2,3);} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda=-1:\ \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 3 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0} \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_2=(1,-1).} \]
Hence $P=\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 4.}\quad The determinant and inverse are
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\det P = (2)(-1)-(1)(3) = -5, \qquad P^{-1} = \frac{1}{-5}\begin{pmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ -3 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}.} \]

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 5.}\quad Multiply in two steps, $AP$ then $P^{-1}(AP)$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{AP = \begin{pmatrix} 8 & -1 \\ 12 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad P^{-1}AP = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 20 & 0 \\ 0 & -5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = D.} \]
The decoupled system is $y_1'=4y_1$, $y_2'=-y_2$.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 6.}\quad Each eigenpair contributes one exponential mode:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\mathbf{x}(t) = c_1 e^{4t}\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} + c_2 e^{-t}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}.} \]
With $\lambda_1=4>0>\lambda_2=-1$ the origin is a saddle.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 7.}\quad Exponentiate the diagonal and reassemble $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{Pe^{Dt} = \begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t} & e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t} & -e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}, \quad e^{At} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t}+3e^{-t} & 2e^{4t}-2e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t}-3e^{-t} & 3e^{4t}+2e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}.} \]
At $t=0$ this is $I$, as it must be.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 8.}\quad Stack the two eigensolutions as columns:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\Psi(t) = \begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t} & e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t} & -e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \det\Psi = -2e^{3t}-3e^{3t} = -5e^{3t} \neq 0.} \]
The nonzero Wronskian confirms the columns are independent.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 9.}\quad Both $\Psi(t)$ and $e^{At}$ solve $X'=AX$; they agree once normalized to the same value at $t=0$. Since $e^{A\cdot 0}=I$,
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{e^{At} = \Psi(t)\,\Psi(0)^{-1}, \qquad \Psi(0) = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = P.} \]
Right-multiplying by $\Psi(0)^{-1}$ forces the value $I$ at $t=0$.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 10.}\quad Here $\det(A-\lambda I)=\lambda^2-4\lambda+13=0$ gives $\lambda=2\pm 3i$. With $\mathbf{v}=(1,-i)$ for $\lambda=2+3i$, Euler's formula yields
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\mathbf{x}(t) = e^{2t}\left[ c_1\begin{pmatrix} \cos 3t \\ \sin 3t \end{pmatrix} + c_2\begin{pmatrix} \sin 3t \\ -\cos 3t \end{pmatrix} \right].} \]
Since $\alpha=2>0$, every trajectory spirals outward.

\medskip

\begin{conceptbox}[Unit Key Takeaway]
One idea threads the whole unit: a linear system is easiest in its own eigenbasis. Recast higher-order ODEs as $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$, then \textcolor{accentorange}{diagonalize $A=PDP^{-1}$} so the modal substitution $\mathbf{y}=P^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ \textcolor{accentorange}{decouples} the system. The \textcolor{accentorange}{matrix exponential $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$} packages every mode into one operator and generalizes scalar $e^{at}$; the \textcolor{accentorange}{fundamental matrix} collects the same solutions and reproduces $e^{At}$ at $t=0$. Complex eigenvalues simply trade pure exponentials for spirals.
\end{conceptbox}

\end{document}
